name: transport
context: MCP transport mechanisms — stdio for local servers, Streamable HTTP with SSE for remote servers
info: This context node covers MCP transport layer details. MCP supports two standard transports: stdio (client launches server as a subprocess, communicates via stdin/stdout with newline-delimited JSON-RPC) and Streamable HTTP (HTTP POST for client-to-server, optional SSE for server-to-client streaming, session management via Mcp-Session-Id header). Stdio is for local servers with zero network overhead and single client. Streamable HTTP is for remote servers supporting multiple clients, OAuth authorization, and resumable streams. Custom transports are permitted if they preserve JSON-RPC framing.
triggers: how do mcp clients connect to servers
set up http transport for mcp
body:
# MCP Transports

The transport layer determines how MCP clients and servers communicate. All transports carry the same JSON-RPC 2.0 messages — the data layer is transport-agnostic.

## stdio Transport

For local servers. The client launches the server as a subprocess and communicates via stdin/stdout.

### How It Works

1. Client spawns the server process
2. Client writes JSON-RPC messages to the server's stdin
3. Server writes JSON-RPC messages to its stdout
4. Messages are newline-delimited, must not contain embedded newlines
5. Server may write logs to stderr (not parsed as MCP messages)
6. Client closes stdin and terminates the subprocess to end the session

### When to Use

- Server runs on the same machine as the client
- No network overhead needed
- Single client per server instance
- Maximum performance for local integrations

### Configuration

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/path/to/dir"]
    },
    "python-server": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["my_server.py"],
      "env": {
        "DATABASE_URL": "postgresql://localhost/mydb"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Python stdio Server

```python
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("my-server")

@mcp.tool()
def hello(name: str) -> str:
    """Say hello."""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run()  # Runs on stdio by default
```

### TypeScript stdio Server

```typescript
import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js";

const server = new McpServer({ name: "my-server", version: "1.0.0" });
const transport = new StdioServerTransport();
await server.connect(transport);
```

## Streamable HTTP Transport

For remote servers. Uses HTTP POST for client-to-server messages with optional Server-Sent Events (SSE) for streaming.

### How It Works

1. Server provides a single HTTP endpoint (e.g., `https://example.com/mcp`)
2. Client sends JSON-RPC messages via HTTP POST
3. Server responds with `application/json` (single response) or `text/event-stream` (SSE stream)
4. Client can GET the endpoint to open an SSE stream for server-initiated messages
5. Session management uses `Mcp-Session-Id` header

### When to Use

- Server runs on a different machine
- Multiple clients need to connect simultaneously
- OAuth authorization is required
- Streaming responses are needed
- Server is a managed/cloud service

### Session Management

1. Server assigns a session ID in the `Mcp-Session-Id` response header during initialization
2. Client includes `Mcp-Session-Id` in all subsequent requests
3. Server returns 404 if the session has expired — client must re-initialize
4. Client sends HTTP DELETE to the endpoint to explicitly end a session

### Protocol Version Header

Clients must include `MCP-Protocol-Version: 2025-06-18` in all HTTP requests after initialization.

### Resumable Streams

Servers can assign `id` fields to SSE events. If a connection drops, the client reconnects with `Last-Event-ID` header and the server replays missed events.

### Configuration

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "remote-service": {
      "url": "https://example.com/mcp",
      "headers": {
        "Authorization": "Bearer <token>"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Security for Streamable HTTP

- Servers **must** validate the `Origin` header to prevent DNS rebinding attacks
- Local servers should bind only to `127.0.0.1`, not `0.0.0.0`
- Implement proper authentication (see [[mcp/auth]])

## Custom Transports

MCP allows custom transports as long as they:
- Preserve JSON-RPC 2.0 message format
- Support bidirectional message exchange
- Implement the MCP lifecycle (initialize, operate, terminate)

## Transport Comparison

| Feature | stdio | Streamable HTTP |
|---------|-------|------------------|
| Deployment | Local subprocess | Remote service |
| Clients | Single | Multiple |
| Network | None | HTTP/HTTPS |
| Streaming | stdout pipe | SSE |
| Auth | Environment-based | OAuth 2.1 / Bearer |
| Performance | Lowest latency | Network latency |
| Session | Process lifetime | Mcp-Session-Id header |
| Resumability | N/A | SSE event IDs |

sections.title: stdio transport
sections.body: Client launches server as a subprocess. Communication via stdin (client→server) and stdout (server→client). Messages are newline-delimited JSON-RPC. Server must not write non-MCP data to stdout. Stderr is available for logging. Best for local integrations — zero network overhead, single client.

sections.title: Streamable HTTP transport
sections.body: Server exposes a single HTTP endpoint. Client sends JSON-RPC via POST. Server responds with JSON or SSE stream. Client can GET to open an SSE stream for server-initiated messages. Session management via `Mcp-Session-Id` header. Supports multiple clients, OAuth auth, and resumable streams. Replaces the deprecated HTTP+SSE transport from protocol version 2024-11-05.

sections.title: Session management
sections.body: For Streamable HTTP: server assigns `Mcp-Session-Id` during initialization. Client includes it in all subsequent requests. Session ID must be cryptographically secure (UUID, JWT, hash). Server returns 404 for expired sessions. Client re-initializes on 404. Client sends DELETE to end a session.

sections.title: Choosing a transport
sections.body: Use stdio when: server is local, single client, maximum performance needed, no auth required. Use Streamable HTTP when: server is remote, multiple clients, OAuth needed, streaming responses, cloud deployment. Custom transports are allowed but must preserve JSON-RPC framing and lifecycle.

links.context: Build clients that connect via these transports

related.note: Architecture explains the two-layer model where transport is the outer layer
Authorization is implemented at the transport layer for HTTP
Servers must choose and configure a transport

author: dojo-community
